1031:});
1032:```
1033:
1034:Alias lists are intentionally conservative v1. Pressure test (§20.3) expands them based on real operator phrasing.
1035:
1036:### 12.3 Handler implementations
1037:
1038:Each handler is a small function calling `canonicalDataService`. For entries whose underlying query doesn't yet exist on `canonicalDataService` (it's currently metrics-only — see audit in brief context), this spec extends `canonicalDataService` with the needed reads:
1039:
1040:```ts
1041:// server/services/canonicalDataService.ts — extensions
1042:
1043:export async function listInactiveContacts(
1044:  args: { orgId: string; subaccountId: string; sinceDaysAgo: number; limit: number }
1045:): Promise<{ rows: CanonicalContact[]; rowCount: number; truncated: boolean }>;
1046:
1047:export async function listStaleOpportunities(
1048:  args: { orgId: string; subaccountId: string; stageKey?: string; staleSince: Date; limit: number }
1049:): Promise<{ rows: CanonicalOpportunity[]; rowCount: number; truncated: boolean }>;
1050:
1051:// ... one extension per registry entry whose data isn't already exposed
1052:```
1053:
1054:Handlers then wrap those into `ExecutorResult`:
1055:
1056:```ts
1057:async function contactsInactiveOverDaysHandler(
1058:  args: CanonicalQueryHandlerArgs
1059:): Promise<ExecutorResult> {
1060:  const sinceDaysAgo = resolveSinceDaysAgo(args.filters, args.dateContext);  // pure helper
1061:  const { rows, rowCount, truncated } = await listInactiveContacts({
1062:    orgId: args.orgId,
1063:    subaccountId: args.subaccountId,
1064:    sinceDaysAgo,
1065:    limit: args.limit,
1066:  });
1067:  return {
1068:    rows,
1069:    rowCount,
1070:    truncated,
1071:    truncationReason: truncated ? 'result_limit' : undefined,
1072:    actualCostCents: 0,  // canonical reads are free
1073:    source: 'canonical',
1074:  };
1075:}
1076:```
1077:
1078:### 12.4 Test plan
1079:
1080:`canonicalQueryRegistry.test.ts`:
1081:
1082:- Every entry's handler invokable with mock `canonicalDataService` returns a valid `ExecutorResult`
1083:- Every entry's `requiredCapabilities` list references existing canonical capabilities (verified against `server/lib/permissions.ts` or equivalent)
1084:- Registry is `Object.freeze`'d — mutation attempts throw in strict mode
1085:
1086:---
1087:
1088:## 13. Live executor
1089:
1090:**File:** `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/executors/liveExecutor.ts`
1091:**Backed by:** `server/services/adapters/ghlReadHelpers.ts` (existing)
1092:
1093:### 13.1 Executor contract
1094:
1095:```ts
1096:export async function executeLive(
1097:  plan: QueryPlan,
1098:  context: ExecutorContext
1099:): Promise<ExecutorResult> {
1100:  if (plan.source !== 'live') {
1101:    throw new Error('liveExecutor dispatched with non-live plan');
1102:  }
1103:  const rateLimiter = getProviderRateLimiter('ghl');
1104:  const release = await rateLimiter.acquire({
1105:    locationId: context.subaccountLocationId,
1106:  });
1107:  try {
1108:    const translated = translateToProviderQuery(plan);  // §13.2
1109:    const response = await ghlReadHelpers.query(translated, context);
1110:    return normaliseLiveResponse(response, plan);
1111:  } finally {
1112:    release();
1113:  }
1114:}
1115:```
1116:
1117:### 13.2 Plan-to-provider translation
1118:
1119:`translateToProviderQuery(plan)` is a pure function in `liveExecutor.ts`. It maps the plan's `filters`, `sort`, `limit`, `projection`, `dateContext` to the GHL endpoint's query parameters. Output is a typed `GhlReadQuery` matching the `ghlReadHelpers` input contract.
1120:
1121:### 13.3 Read-only enforcement (static gate)
1122:
1123:`liveExecutor.ts` imports `ghlReadHelpers` (read-only module) — it does NOT import `ghlAdapter`'s write methods (`createContact`, `createTask`, etc.). v1 enforces this via a new repo-style static gate: `scripts/verify-crm-query-planner-read-only.sh` greps every file under `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/executors/` for forbidden imports against `server/adapters/ghlAdapter` (write methods) and exits 1 if any are found. The gate is added to `scripts/run-all-gates.sh`.
1124:
1125:This matches the convention the repo already uses for `verify-no-direct-adapter-calls.sh` and the other 24 `verify-*.sh` gates — it is a CI-level static check, not a compile-time TypeScript check. Attempted write imports fail the static gate at CI time, not at runtime.
1126:
1127:Even if an LLM-generated `QueryPlan` contained a malicious write-like structure, the live executor has no way to reach a mutation method — the write function is not imported and the static gate prevents one from being added without being noticed.
1128:
1129:### 13.4 Result-size cap
1130:
1131:Default `limit` is 100 rows. If GHL's response exceeds the plan's limit, truncation is applied client-side with `truncated: true` and `truncationReason: 'result_limit'`.
1132:
1133:### 13.5 Rate-limit behaviour
1134:
1135:The `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl').acquire()` call queues if the per-location bucket is full. ClientPulse polling and planner live queries share the same bucket — the rate limiter's scheduler (already shipped) ensures fair-queueing so neither starves the other. The planner does not set a timeout on the acquire itself; if the queue depth is pathological, the eventual response will be cost-exceeded or rate-limited depending on which limit fires first.
1136:
1137:### 13.6 Test plan
1138:
1139:`liveExecutor.test.ts` (integration, mocked `ghlReadHelpers`):
1140:
1141:- Plan translation is deterministic per input
1142:- Rate limiter is acquired + released (even on thrown error)
1143:- Truncation fires at `rows.length > plan.limit`
1144:- Non-live plan throws before any adapter call
1145:- Adapter error (401, 429, 5xx) surfaces as structured error with appropriate `errorCode`
1146:
1147:---
1148:
1149:## 14. Hybrid executor
1150:
1151:**File:** `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/executors/hybridExecutor.ts`
1152:**Pattern:** `canonical_base_with_live_filter` — the **only** v1 hybrid pattern.
1153:
1154:### 14.1 Executor contract
1155:
1156:```ts
1157:export async function executeHybrid(
1158:  plan: QueryPlan,
1159:  context: ExecutorContext
1160:): Promise<ExecutorResult> {
1161:  if (plan.source !== 'hybrid') {
1162:    throw new Error('hybridExecutor dispatched with non-hybrid plan');
1163:  }
1164:  if (plan.hybridPattern !== 'canonical_base_with_live_filter') {
1165:    throw new Error(`unsupported hybrid pattern: ${plan.hybridPattern}`);
1166:  }
1167:  // 1. Split plan into canonical base + live filter
1168:  const { canonicalBase, liveFilter } = splitHybridPlan(plan);
1169:  // 2. Run canonical base
1170:  const baseResult = await executeCanonical(canonicalBase, context);
1171:  // 3. Run live filter against baseResult.rows (in-memory, one request per unique row group)
1172:  const filtered = await applyLiveFilter(baseResult.rows, liveFilter, context);
1173:  // 4. Merge + truncate
1174:  return mergeHybridResults(baseResult, filtered, plan);
1175:}
1176:```
1177:
1178:### 14.2 Constraint enforcement (reiterated from validator §11.2)
1179:
1180:The validator guarantees at entry:
1181:
1182:- Plan has exactly one "live filter" (one filter referencing a live-only field per schemaContext)
1183:- Other filters are canonical-resolvable
1184:- `limit` ≤ canonical base's native limit
1185:- `primaryEntity` matches a registered canonical entry
1186:
1187:Executor does not re-validate; if any of these invariants are violated, it throws (defensive runtime check against an unvalidated plan somehow reaching the executor).
1188:
1189:### 14.3 Live-filter application
1190:
1191:`applyLiveFilter` fetches only the live-only field for each row in `baseResult.rows`, not a general re-query. This bounds work to `O(rowCount)` live calls at worst — with the result-size cap at 100 rows, that's bounded. In practice, batch endpoints (e.g. "fetch tags for these N contact IDs") let us reduce to `O(1)` or `O(ceil(rowCount / batch_size))` calls.
1192:
1193:**Hard cap:** if a hybrid query would require more than 10 live calls (after batching), the executor returns `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` with a suggestion to narrow the canonical base first. This is the circuit breaker for the combinatorial-work risk the brief's §6.3 warned about.
1194:
1195:### 14.4 Test plan
1196:
1197:`hybridExecutor.test.ts`:
1198:
1199:- Canonical + live merge produces rows that appear in both
1200:- Live filter reducing-only semantics (no rows added post-merge)
1201:- 10-live-call cap triggers `cost_exceeded` error
1202:- Non-matching pattern throws before dispatch
1203:- Canonical base failure surfaces cleanly (doesn't attempt live)
1204:- Live failure surfaces cleanly (doesn't corrupt canonical base)
1205:
1206:---
1207:
1208:## 15. Result normaliser + approval card generation
1209:
1210:**Files:** `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/resultNormaliser.ts` + `resultNormaliserPure.ts` + `approvalCardGeneratorPure.ts`
1211:
1212:### 15.1 Normaliser contract
1213:
1214:```ts
1215:export function normaliseToArtefacts(
1216:  plan: QueryPlan,
1217:  execResult: ExecutorResult,
1218:  context: NormaliserContext
1219:): { structured: BriefStructuredResult; approvalCards: BriefApprovalCard[] } {
1220:  const structured = buildStructuredResult(plan, execResult);
1221:  const approvalCards = generateApprovalCards(plan, execResult, context);
1222:  return { structured, approvalCards };
1223:}
1224:```
1225:
1226:`buildStructuredResult` and `generateApprovalCards` are pure and separately testable.
1227:
1228:### 15.2 `BriefStructuredResult` construction
1229:
1230:Follows the committed contract (`shared/types/briefResultContract.ts`) verbatim. Key fields populated:
1231:
1232:| Field | Source |
1233:|---|---|
1234:| `kind` | Literal `'structured'` |
1235:| `summary` | Templated from plan + rowCount (e.g. `"{{rowCount}} contacts inactive 30d"`) |
1236:| `entityType` | `plan.primaryEntity` |
1237:| `filtersApplied` | Every filter from `plan.filters` rendered as a chip with `humanLabel` |
1238:| `rows` | `execResult.rows` (shape varies per entity) |
1239:| `rowCount` | `execResult.rowCount` |
1240:| `truncated` | `execResult.truncated` |
1241:| `truncationReason` | `execResult.truncationReason` |
1242:| `suggestions` | Generated from plan context (see §15.3) |
1243:| `costCents` | `execResult.actualCostCents` |
1244:| `source` | `execResult.source` |
1245:
1246:### 15.3 Suggestion generation
1247:
1248:`generateSuggestions(plan, execResult)` emits refinement affordances. Rules:
1249:
1250:- **If truncated:** suggest narrowing by date, owner, stage, or tag.
1251:- **If rowCount > 50:** suggest sort by a relevant field (last activity, created date, amount).
1252:- **If entity has common follow-ups:** suggest those (e.g. contacts → "Email these contacts").
1253:- **All suggestions must be re-parseable as a new Brief** — full instruction strings, not fragments. Per brief §7 rule.
1254:
1255:Suggestions are a pure function of plan + result; no LLM call.
1256:
1257:### 15.4 Approval card generation
1258:
1259:Per brief §11.8 — one illustrative example ships v1: sending an email to a **single contact** from the returned set. The registered `crm.send_email` action schema (`server/config/actionRegistry.ts`) expects exactly one `toContactId` plus a concrete `from` / `subject` / `body`, so the v1 card is one-contact-at-a-time. Batch-email (one approval, N sends) requires extending the action registry with a new batch slug — that's tracked in the Deferred Items section, not v1 scope.
1260:
1261:```ts
1262:export function generateApprovalCards(
1263:  plan: QueryPlan,
1264:  execResult: ExecutorResult,
1265:  context: NormaliserContext
1266:): BriefApprovalCard[] {
1267:  const cards: BriefApprovalCard[] = [];
1268:
1269:  // v1 pattern: contact-list result → single-contact email follow-up for the top row.
1270:  // The UI can cycle through other rows to generate additional cards, but each card
1271:  // maps 1:1 to a dispatchable `crm.send_email` action.
1272:  if (plan.primaryEntity === 'contacts' && execResult.rows.length > 0) {
1273:    const top = execResult.rows[0];
1274:    const toContactId = String(top.id);
1275:    cards.push({
1276:      kind: 'approval',
1277:      summary: `Send email to ${top.displayName ?? toContactId}`,
1278:      actionSlug: 'crm.send_email',
1279:      actionArgs: {
1280:        from: context.defaultSenderIdentifier,       // resolved from subaccount config at card-build time
1281:        toContactId,
1282:        subject: '',                                 // user fills at approval time
1283:        body: '',                                    // user fills at approval time
1284:        scheduleHint: 'immediate',
1285:      },
1286:      affectedRecordIds: [toContactId],
1287:      riskLevel: 'low',                              // single-contact email — never more than 'low' in v1
1288:    });
1289:  }
1290:
1291:  // Extension points for future cards (not v1):
1292:  // - opportunities → "Update stage for selected"
1293:  // - tasks → "Reassign selected tasks"
1294:  // - appointments → "Reschedule selected"
1295:  // - contacts (batch) → "Send email to N contacts" — requires new batch action slug (Deferred Items)
1296:
1297:  return cards;
1298:}
1299:```
1300:
1301:`context.defaultSenderIdentifier` is a new `NormaliserContext` field populated from the caller's permission context (see §16.4) — it's the CRM-connected sender identifier for the subaccount (existing convention in `crm.send_email` dispatch paths). If the subaccount has no connected sender, the card generator skips email-card emission.
1302:
1303:Approval cards are **suggestions only** — the chat surface renders them; the user fills `subject`/`body` and approves; the approval creates a normal review-gated action via `actionService`. The planner does not execute the action. The `actionArgs` emitted by the generator MUST satisfy the registered action's `parameterSchema` — the registered schema is the source of truth, this generator follows it.
1304:
1305:### 15.5 Test plan
1306:
1307:`resultNormaliserPure.test.ts`:
1308:
1309:- Every `BriefStructuredResult` field populated correctly for canonical, live, hybrid sources
1310:- `filtersApplied` matches plan filters exactly
1311:- Truncation propagates
1312:
1313:`approvalCardGeneratorPure.test.ts`:
1314:
1315:- Contact-list with ≥1 row → single-contact email card for the top row
1316:- Contact-list with 0 rows → no card
1317:- Opportunity-list → no card in v1
1318:- Subaccount missing `defaultSenderIdentifier` → no card (graceful skip)
1319:- Emitted `actionArgs` satisfy the registered `crm.send_email` zod schema (verified against a test-time copy of the action registry)
1320:- `actionSlug` is a registered skill
1321:
1322:---
1323:
1324:## 16. Governance integration
1325:
1326:No new governance primitives. The planner wires into existing systems.
1327:
1328:### 16.1 `llmRouter.routeCall` (mandatory)
1329:
1330:All Stage 3 LLM calls route through `llmRouter.routeCall`. This is enforced by the repo-wide static gate `scripts/verify-no-direct-adapter-calls.sh` (exit 1 on direct provider imports) plus the runtime `assertCalledFromRouter()` check inside adapters. The planner has no bypass — and must not attempt one.
1331:
1332:The call parameters lock:
1333:
1334:```ts
1335:{
1336:  task: 'crm_query_planner',
1337:  context: {
1338:    sourceType: 'system',
1339:    sourceId: 'crm_query_planner',
1340:    featureTag: 'crm-query-planner',
1341:    systemCallerPolicy: 'strict',
1342:    orgId, subaccountId, runId,
1343:  },
1344:  model: resolvePlannerTier(orgId, escalate),
1345:  // ...
1346:}
1347:```
1348:
1349:`sourceType: 'system'` + `systemCallerPolicy: 'strict'` means the router applies the system caller's strictest routing policy (no user-override model selection, no per-user budget deviations).
1350:
1351:### 16.2 `runCostBreaker`
1352:
1353:Every planner invocation runs inside a `runId` context (provided by the caller). `runCostBreaker`'s existing `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` is called before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch — the planner does not need new budget logic. If the run's ledger sum exceeds the ceiling, the call throws and the planner surfaces `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }`.
1354:
1355:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.
1356:
1357:### 16.3 Rate limiter
1358:
1359:`getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')` acquired in the live executor (§13.5). Per-location keying matches ClientPulse polling's keying, so the two share the same bucket and fair-queue together.
1360:
1361:Per brief §11.5, a per-subaccount rate-limit budget is deferred to v2 unless real traffic signals abuse.
1362:
1363:### 16.4 RLS / principal context
1364:
1365:Every canonical read happens inside `withPrincipalContext(context.orgId, context.subaccountId, context.userId, async () => { ... })`. This is existing repo convention — any query outside principal context leaks cross-subaccount data. The planner's caller (`crmQueryPlannerService`) wraps the entire pipeline in this context before dispatching to any executor.
1366:
1367:Live reads inherit context via the OAuth token scoped to the subaccount's connected provider — the adapter loads credentials per subaccount, so cross-subaccount leakage is structurally impossible.
1368:
1369:### 16.5 Audit
1370:
1371:Each planner invocation emits a normal run event stream consumed by Agent Live Execution Logs (`agent_execution_events` table, shipped P1). The event set in §17 integrates with existing run-trace infrastructure; no new audit tables.
1372:
1373:### 16.6 Read-only guarantees (two independent guards)
1374:
1375:1. **Validator** (§11.2) rejects any plan with write-shaped operators or mutation affinity.
1376:2. **Executor layer** imports only read helpers from the adapter surface — enforced by `scripts/verify-crm-query-planner-read-only.sh` as a CI-level static gate (§13.3). No write helpers are in scope at module-load time.
1377:
1378:These are independent — a regression in one does not open a hole in the other. Both are CI-level checks (one runtime-in-test via the validator's pure tests, one static-grep via the gate script); neither is a compile-time TypeScript check.
1379:
1380:---
1381:
1382:## 17. Observability
1383:
1384:All events emitted via `plannerEvents.emit(event)` which:
1385:
1386:1. Writes a structured log line at `info` level (or `warn` for errors).
1387:2. Calls `tryEmitAgentEvent` (from `server/services/agentExecutionEventEmitter.ts`) so the event surfaces in Agent Live Execution Logs (Phase 1 just shipped).
1388:3. Increments in-memory metrics counters (for the derived metrics in §17.2).
1389:
1390:### 17.1 Event payloads
1391:
1392:| Kind | Payload |
1393:|---|---|
1394:| `planner.stage1_matched` | `{ registryKey, intentHash }` |
1395:| `planner.stage1_missed` | `{ intentHash }` |
1396:| `planner.stage2_cache_hit` | `{ intentHash, cachedAt, hitCount }` |
1397:| `planner.stage2_cache_miss` | `{ intentHash }` |
1398:| `planner.stage3_parse_started` | `{ intentHash, modelTier, schemaTokens }` |
1399:| `planner.stage3_parse_completed` | `{ intentHash, modelTier, inputTokens, outputTokens, latencyMs, confidence }` |
1400:| `planner.stage3_escalated` | `{ intentHash, fromTier, toTier, reason: 'low_confidence' \| 'hybrid_detected' \| 'large_schema' }` |
1401:| `planner.validation_failed` | `{ intentHash, rejectedRule, rejectedValue }` |
1402:| `planner.classified` | `{ intentHash, source, intentClass, confidence, stageResolved, canonicalCandidateKey }` |
1403:| `planner.executor_dispatched` | `{ intentHash, executor: 'canonical'\|'live'\|'hybrid', predictedCostCents }` |
1404:| `planner.canonical_promoted` | `{ intentHash, fromSource: 'live', toSource: 'canonical' \| 'hybrid', registryKey }` |
1405:| `planner.result_emitted` | `{ intentHash, artefactKind: 'structured'\|'approval'\|'error', rowCount, truncated, actualCostCents, stageResolved }` |
1406:| `planner.error_emitted` | `{ intentHash, errorCode, rejectedRule? }` |
1407:
1408:Every event carries `{ kind, at, orgId, subaccountId, runId?, intentHash }` as standard envelope.
1409:
1410:### 17.2 Derived metrics
1411:
1412:Computed from the event stream (batch, not per-event):
1413:
1414:- **`planner.llm_skipped_rate`** = `(stage1_matched + stage2_cache_hit) / total_queries`. North-star metric for deterministic-coverage optimisation.
1415:- **`planner.canonical_hit_rate`** = `canonical_classified / total_queries`.
1416:- **`planner.hybrid_unsupported_rate`** = `count(classified.source === 'hybrid' AND error_emitted.errorCode === 'unsupported_query' for the same intentHash) / count(total classified events)`. Numerator uses the `planner.classified` → `planner.error_emitted` pairing on the shared `intentHash`; denominator is total `planner.classified` events in the batch window. When this climbs, add a new hybrid pattern.
1417:- **`planner.avg_query_cost_cents`** — mean `actualCostCents`, segmented by `stageResolved` (1, 2, 3).
1418:- **`planner.avg_stage3_latency_ms`** — mean LLM Stage 3 `latencyMs`.
1419:- **`planner.escalation_rate`** — `stage3_escalated / stage3_parse_completed`.
1420:- **`planner.validation_failure_rate`** — `validation_failed / stage3_parse_completed`.
1421:
1422:Metrics surface through the existing system-pnl admin route (`/system/llm-pnl`) with a new subsection under "Task-class breakdown." No new dashboard.
1423:
1424:### 17.3 Query Memory Layer alignment
1425:
1426:Per brief §11.12, the event shape above is **deliberately consumable** by the future Query Memory Layer. Specifically:
1427:
1428:- `intentHash` is the primary key for grouping queries.
1429:- `stage2_cache_hit.hitCount` identifies hot queries.
1430:- `executor_dispatched.executor = 'live'` + high frequency = canonical promotion candidate.
1431:- `validation_failed` + high frequency = alias-list or registry gap.
1432:
1433:No v1 surfacing. No promotion workflow. Data captured for v2 to consume.
1434:
1435:---
1436:
1437:## 18. API surface
1438:
1439:### 18.1 New route
1440:
1441:```
1442:POST /api/crm-query-planner/query
1443:```
1444:
1445:Request body:
1446:
1447:```ts
1448:{
1449:  rawIntent: string;
1450:  briefContext: {
1451:    orgId: string;
1452:    subaccountId: string;
1453:    userId: string;
1454:    runId?: string;       // attached when called mid-run
1455:    briefId?: string;     // from the Brief surface, when applicable
1456:  };
1457:}
1458:```
1459:
1460:Response body:
1461:
1462:```ts
1463:{
1464:  artefacts: BriefChatArtefact[];   // from shared/types/briefResultContract.ts
1465:  costPreview: BriefCostPreview;
1466:  stageResolved: 1 | 2 | 3;
1467:  intentHash: string;               // for client-side dedup / follow-up Briefs
1468:}
1469:```
1470:
1471:Authentication: `authenticate` middleware (existing).
1472:Permission gate at the route level: caller must have **at least one CRM read capability** on the target subaccount (any of `canonical.contacts.read` / `canonical.opportunities.read` / `canonical.appointments.read` / `canonical.conversations.read` / `canonical.revenue.read` / `canonical.tasks.read` / `clientpulse.health_snapshots.read`). The coarse route gate rejects obvious attackers without CRM access; per-entry capability enforcement happens inside the canonical executor (§12.1) once the plan resolves. This two-layer design avoids pre-classifying intent just to pick the right route gate.
1473:
1474:The edge check is scoped to the target subaccount via `resolveSubaccount(subaccountId, orgId)` (existing convention).
1475:
1476:### 18.2 New skill in `actionRegistry.ts`
1477:
1478:```ts
1479:{
1480:  slug: 'crm.query',
1481:  namespace: 'crm',
1482:  category: 'crm_exploration',
1483:  gateLevel: 'none',                          // read-only
1484:  idempotencyStrategy: 'read_only',
1485:  parameterSchema: z.object({
1486:    rawIntent: z.string().min(3).max(2000),
1487:  }),
1488:  readOnlyHint: true,
1489:  destructiveHint: false,
1490:  openWorldHint: true,
1491:  description: 'Answer a free-text CRM question using the CRM Query Planner.',
1492:  handler: async (args, context) => {
1493:    return runQuery(args.rawIntent, {
1494:      orgId: context.orgId,
1495:      subaccountId: context.subaccountId,
1496:      userId: context.userId,
1497:      runId: context.runId,
1498:    });
1499:  },
1500:}
1501:```
1502:
1503:Agent-facing tool. Exposed to any agent whose `capabilityMap` grants `crm.query`. The MCP server's tool catalogue auto-picks this up (existing pattern from `server/mcp/mcpServer.ts`).
1504:
1505:### 18.3 No client-side route
1506:
1507:v1 relies on the Brief-surface branch's chat surface rendering `BriefChatArtefact`s. No client changes here.
1508:
1509:A stopgap "Ask CRM" panel is optional (brief §11). This spec does not implement it — if sequencing requires a standalone UI before the Brief surface lands, a minimal panel can be built as a follow-on P2 task by consuming the same route and renderers used by the Brief surface.
1510:
1511:---
1512:
1513:## 19. Phased delivery plan
1514:
1515:The spec ships in three phases on this branch. Each phase is its own commit cluster, green tests before the next phase starts.
1516:
1517:### Phase P1 — Deterministic core (no LLM)
1518:
1519:**What ships:** the deterministic part of the planner. Stages 1, 2, 4; canonical executor; canonical registry with all 8 entries; result normaliser; API route; skill registration. **Stage 3 is stubbed** — on any intent that misses Stage 1 and Stage 2, the stub short-circuits to a `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'unsupported_query', suggestions: [...] }` **before** Stage 4 runs. The cache module (§9) is shipped but its write path is never exercised in P1 because only Stage 3 successes are cached and Stage 3 produces none. Stage 2 reads are wired so P2's first successful Stage 3 can populate the cache without further plumbing.
1520:
1521:**Why this phase first:** the hardest-to-get-right pieces are the deterministic paths (registry matching, validation, normalisation). Proving them work without LLM noise in the loop is the highest-leverage way to start. Any user-visible failure at this phase is deterministic and fixable.
1522:
1523:**Files:**
1524:- `shared/types/crmQueryPlanner.ts`
1525:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/normaliseIntentPure.ts` + test
1526:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/registryMatcherPure.ts` + test
1527:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/planCache.ts` + `planCachePure.ts` + test
1528:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/validatePlanPure.ts` + test
1529:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/executors/canonicalQueryRegistry.ts` + test
1530:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/executors/canonicalExecutor.ts`
1531:- `server/services/canonicalDataService.ts` (extensions for each registry handler)
1532:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/resultNormaliser.ts` + `resultNormaliserPure.ts` + test
1533:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/approvalCardGeneratorPure.ts` + test
1534:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/crmQueryPlannerService.ts` (orchestration, with Stage 3 stub)
1535:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/plannerEvents.ts`
1536:- `server/routes/crmQueryPlanner.ts`
1537:- `server/config/actionRegistry.ts` extension (register `crm.query`)
1538:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/index.ts`
1539:
1540:**Exit criteria:**
1541:- All 8 canonical registry entries resolvable via Stage 1 matcher
1542:- All registry handlers return valid `ExecutorResult`
1543:- Result normaliser emits spec-compliant `BriefStructuredResult`
1544:- One approval card path covered (`crm.send_email` on contact results)
1545:- Pure test coverage: every `*Pure.ts` file has ≥1 test file with the cases listed in the relevant section
1546:- `POST /api/crm-query-planner/query` returns correct artefact for all 8 registry-matched queries
1547:
1548:### Phase P2 — LLM fallback + live executor
1549:
1550:**What ships:** Stage 3 LLM planner; schema-context service; live executor; updated orchestration so Stage 3 actually runs.
1551:
1552:**Hybrid handling in P2:** the hybrid executor (§14) does NOT ship until P3. Until P3, Stage 4's hybrid-pattern rule (§11.2 rule 7) still runs to detect the shape, but any plan with `source: 'hybrid'` is rewritten by `crmQueryPlannerService` into a `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'unsupported_query', suggestions: [...'ships in P3...'] }` before executor dispatch. This keeps Stage 4's shape-check logic exercised during P2 without requiring the executor half.
1553:
1554:**Why this phase second:** with the deterministic core proven, adding the LLM fallback is scope-contained. Live executor depends on Stage 3 producing valid live plans, so they ship together.
1555:
1556:**Files added:**
1557:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/llmPlanner.ts`
1558:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/llmPlannerPromptPure.ts` + test
1559:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/schemaContextService.ts`
1560:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/schemaContextPure.ts` + test
1561:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/executors/liveExecutor.ts` + test
1562:
1563:**Files updated:**
1564:- `crmQueryPlannerService.ts` (wire Stage 3 + live executor)
1565:
1566:**Exit criteria:**
1567:- Stage 3 produces valid `DraftQueryPlan` for at least 10 intents outside the registry
1568:- Validator accepts valid plans and rejects invalid ones
1569:- Escalation fires on low confidence and on hybrid detection
1570:- Live executor returns correct `ExecutorResult` for at least 5 long-tail queries
1571:- Rate limiter acquired + released correctly in all success + error paths
1572:- Per-query cent ceiling enforced
1573:
1574:### Phase P3 — Hybrid + observability surfacing
1575:
1576:**What ships:** hybrid executor; full observability metrics exposed in system-pnl admin view; any remaining approval card patterns surfaced from pressure-test results.
1577:
1578:**Files added:**
1579:- `server/services/crmQueryPlanner/executors/hybridExecutor.ts` + test
1580:
1581:**Files updated:**
1582:- `server/services/systemPnlService.ts` (add planner metrics subsection)
1583:- `client/src/pages/SystemPnlPage.tsx` or equivalent (render the new subsection)
1584:
1585:**Exit criteria:**
1586:- Hybrid pattern `canonical_base_with_live_filter` returns correct results for 3+ real pressure-test queries
1587:- 10-live-call cap fires on pathological hybrid
1588:- Metrics visible in system-pnl admin view
1589:- `planner.llm_skipped_rate` measurable over a 24h window
1590:
1591:### 19.1 What does NOT get its own phase
1592:
1593:- Schema changes: **none in v1.** If real traffic signals a need (plan-cache persistence, audit escalation), that's a separate spec.
1594:- Query Memory Layer promotion surface: separate feature, separate brief.
1595:- External MCP exposure: deferred (brief §10).
1596:- Non-GHL adapters: each provider is its own follow-on feature reusing the planner layer.
1597:
1598:---
1599:
1600:## 20. Test plan
1601:
1602:### 20.1 Pure tests
1603:
1604:Every `*Pure.ts` module ships with a sibling `*Pure.test.ts`. Spec already listed per-module cases in §7–§15. Summary:
1605:
1606:| File | Minimum cases |
1607:|---|---|
1608:| `normaliseIntentPure.test.ts` | 15 |
1609:| `registryMatcherPure.test.ts` | ~40 (35 aliases × 1 hit + ~5 edge cases — empty intent, malformed intent, collision detection, non-aliased hash, `QueryPlan` shape) |
1610:| `planCachePure.test.ts` | 5 |
1611:| `llmPlannerPromptPure.test.ts` | 4 |
1612:| `validatePlanPure.test.ts` | 20 |
1613:| `schemaContextPure.test.ts` | 8 |
1614:| `resultNormaliserPure.test.ts` | 12 |
1615:| `approvalCardGeneratorPure.test.ts` | 6 |
1616:| `canonicalQueryRegistry.test.ts` | 1 per entry (8) + 2 structural tests |
1617:
1618:Total: ~115 pure test cases. Each case is a single input → output assertion; nothing involves I/O, HTTP, or DB.
1619:
1620:### 20.2 Integration tests
1621:
1622:Testing posture for this spec follows the repo's static-gates-primary / pure-tests-secondary convention. The only carved-out integration test is RLS isolation — a genuinely hot-path cross-tenant correctness concern that can't be proven by pure tests alone. Everything else that was previously an integration test here is covered by pure tests against mocked primitives.
1623:
1624:`server/services/crmQueryPlanner/__tests__/integration.test.ts` (single integration file, RLS-only):
1625:
1626:- RLS: subaccount-A caller cannot see subaccount-B data (explicitly assert cross-subaccount isolation). Uses the existing `rls.context-propagation.test.ts` harness pattern — the repo's accepted integration-harness primitive for RLS.
1627:
1628:The previously-listed end-to-end, cost-breaker, and rate-limiter cases move to pure tests elsewhere:
1629:
1630:- End-to-end (registry-matched + fall-through) → covered by `crmQueryPlannerService.test.ts` (pure, with mocked registry / cache / llmRouter / executors).
1631:- Cost breaker behaviour → covered by `validatePlanPure.test.ts` + `crmQueryPlannerService.test.ts` with a mocked `runCostBreaker` returning a ledger-exceeded signal; asserts `cost_exceeded` surfaces without executor dispatch.
1632:- Rate limiter acquire/release lifecycle → covered by `liveExecutor.test.ts` with a mocked `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')`; asserts acquire on entry and release on both success and error paths.
1633:
1634:### 20.3 Pressure test (manual — brief §13)
1635:
1636:**Before P2 ships.** Architect runs the 10–15 real query pressure test from brief §13:
1637:
1638:1. Collect 10–15 real queries from ops notes / founder journal / support tickets. Not LLM-generated.
1639:2. For each, assert:
1640:   - Which stage resolves it?
1641:   - If Stage 1: does the alias list cover the phrasing? If not, add aliases to the registry.
1642:   - If Stage 3: does the LLM produce a plan the validator accepts? If not, tune prompt or schema context.
1643:   - If hybrid: does it fit the `canonical_base_with_live_filter` pattern? If not, triage — is this a second pattern worth shipping, or is "unsupported" the right answer?
1644:   - If unsupported: are the `suggestions[]` genuinely constructive?
1645:
1646:3. Record results in `tasks/builds/crm-query-planner/pressure-test-results.md` (new file).
1647:4. Any alias additions, synonym additions, or prompt tunings made as a result commit together with the P2 code changes.
1648:
1649:### 20.4 Gate coverage
1650:
1651:Must pass before merge to main:
1652:
1653:- `npm run lint` — no errors
1654:- `npm run typecheck` — no errors
1655:- `npm test -- crm-query-planner` — all pure + integration tests green
1656:- `scripts/verify-no-direct-adapter-calls.sh` — no direct provider imports
1657:- `scripts/run-all-gates.sh` — clean
1658:
1659:`pr-reviewer` run required before PR. `spec-reviewer` run on this spec **before** P1 starts (see §21 open questions).
1660:
1661:---
1662:
1663:## 21. Rollout + feature flags
1664:
1665:### 21.1 No feature flag v1
1666:
1667:Per `CLAUDE.md` rule: "No feature flags or backwards-compatibility shims when you can just change the code." The planner is a new subsystem; there's no prior shape to toggle off.
1668:
1669:Access is gated by the existing skill-permission system: agents need `crm.query` in their `capabilityMap` to invoke. Orgs not yet onboarded to the planner simply don't grant the capability.
1670:
1671:### 21.2 System-settings for tier config
1672:
1673:Added to `systemSettings` table (existing — no schema change, just new rows):
1674:
1675:| Key | Default | Purpose |
1676:|---|---|---|
1677:| `crm_query_planner_default_tier` | `'haiku'` | Stage 3 default model |
1678:| `crm_query_planner_escalation_tier` | `'sonnet'` | Escalation model |
1679:| `crm_query_planner_confidence_threshold` | `0.6` | Below this = escalate |
1680:| `crm_query_planner_per_query_cents` | `100` | Per-query cent ceiling |
1681:| `crm_query_planner_schema_tokens_default` | `2000` | Schema-context token budget |
1682:| `crm_query_planner_schema_tokens_escalated` | `4000` | Budget during escalation |
1683:
1684:Per-org override: `organisation_hierarchies.operational_config.crm_query_planner = { ... }` if present overrides system defaults. Already-existing pattern — no new config wiring needed.
1685:
1686:### 21.3 Phased rollout per org
1687:
1688:This is **capability-grant rollout**, not infrastructure-level staged rollout. Code ships to all instances simultaneously on P3 merge — there is no traffic-shifting, no feature flag, no per-org code path, and no canary deploy. Rollout here means onboarding orgs to the planner one at a time via the existing skill-permission system (granting `crm.query` in an org's `capabilityMap`), which is standard operational practice and not in tension with the codebase's "no staged rollout" posture.
1689:
1690:Once P3 ships on main, orgs are onboarded one at a time:
1691:
1692:1. Grant `crm.query` capability to the org's Orchestrator agent.
1693:2. Run pressure-test queries against that org's subaccount to verify real-data performance.
1694:3. Expand to other agents in the org as confidence grows.
1695:4. `planner.llm_skipped_rate` per org visible in system-pnl; if it's degrading for a specific org, investigate before broader rollout.
1696:
1697:No automatic rollout — each org is a conscious decision based on traffic and data shape.
1698:
1699:---
1700:
1701:## 22. Open questions for implementation
1702:
1703:Tracked as `// TODO(spec-open-N)` in code; surfaced at PR review time.
1704:
1705:1. **`canonicalDataService` extensions — drizzle query shape.** Each new handler (e.g. `listInactiveContacts`) needs the exact drizzle query. Handler body shown in §12.3 is directional; the implementer confirms the current schema's column names and indexes during P1. If any column the handler needs is missing, that's an open question, not a blocker — the spec falls through to live for that query class until canonical catches up.
1706:2. **~~Dependency-cruiser rule for read-only enforcement.~~ Closed.** v1 ships `scripts/verify-crm-query-planner-read-only.sh` as a repo-style static gate (see §13.3 / §16.6). Matches the existing `verify-*.sh` convention; no dependency-cruiser adoption required.
1707:3. **Stage 2 cache persistence.** v1 is in-process; process restart loses the cache. Open question: should we persist to Redis for multi-instance deployments? Decision point: once we observe `planner.llm_skipped_rate` drift across instances, not before. The hit-rate metric is the trigger.
1708:4. **Schema-context compression — concrete algorithm.** §11.11 specifies "entity-level summaries + top-N fields per entity + per-query filtering." The exact summarisation algorithm (top-N by what? frequency? alphabetical?) is deferred to implementation — initial cut is static-ranked (most commonly-used fields per entity, hardcoded list); pressure-test results inform whether to replace with frequency-weighted.
1709:5. **LLM intent-parse timeout.** How long before Stage 3 is abandoned? No explicit v1 timeout specified; `llmRouter` has its own default. Open question: does the planner need a stricter override? Recommend no — router defaults are tuned for the cost envelope and we don't want to introduce a second timeout source.
1710:6. **Per-run cost ledger attribution for planner calls.** The planner's Stage 3 call is a normal `llmRouter` call and lands in the ledger automatically. Open question: do we need a `featureTag: 'crm-query-planner'` dimension in the per-run cost panel so users can see "of this run's cost, X cents came from CRM queries"? Recommend yes — small extension, high ops value.
1711:
1712:---
1713:
1714:## Deferred Items
1715:
1716:Consolidated list of every v1 exclusion with an explicit verdict. Authoring-checklist §7 requires a single `## Deferred Items` section; the items below are the canonical source. References in §1.2, §1.3, §2.1, §9.2, §15.4, §17, §22 point here.
1717:
1718:Verdict legend:
1719:- **DEFER-V2** — known follow-on scope; will be picked up in a dedicated follow-on when the trigger fires
1720:- **WON'T-DO** — explicit non-goal, not planned to ship regardless of signal
1721:- **BUILD-WHEN-SIGNAL** — ship only when a named metric or real incident justifies it
1722:
1723:| Item | Verdict | Trigger / Reason |
1724:|---|---|---|
1725:| Hybrid patterns beyond `canonical_base_with_live_filter` (§1.2) | BUILD-WHEN-SIGNAL | `planner.hybrid_unsupported_rate` climbs AND pressure-test (§20.3) surfaces a second pattern worth codifying |
1726:| Semantic / fuzzy plan-cache match (§1.2) | BUILD-WHEN-SIGNAL | Exact-match `planner.llm_skipped_rate` plateaus below target before alias coverage exhausts — i.e. deterministic coverage is blocked by phrasing drift, not missing aliases |
1727:| Query Memory Layer surfacing (§1.2, §17.3) | DEFER-V2 | Owned by a follow-on spec — Layer's promotion dashboard + workflow. Event shape is v1-locked so v2 can consume without migration |
1728:| Non-GHL live executors (HubSpot, Salesforce, Pipedrive) (§1.2) | DEFER-V2 | Same planner, new adapter — ships when a second CRM integration is in-scope; no v1 work |
1729:| Agent-facing `crm.schema_describe` skill (§1.2, §2.1 brief §11.3) | WON'T-DO (unless signal) | Automatic Stage 3 injection makes a round-trip skill redundant; revisit only if schema injection cost exceeds the per-query ceiling |
1730:| Brief-surface export / saved-segment affordances (§1.2) | DEFER-V2 | Owned by the Brief-surface branch |
1731:| External MCP exposure of the planner (§1.2) | WON'T-DO | Architectural non-goal — planner is an internal primitive; external agents hit the Brief surface |
1732:| Universal Brief chat surface (§1.2) | DEFER-V2 | Owned by a separate branch |
1733:| Free-text writes via the planner (§1.2) | WON'T-DO | Writes remain review-gated `crm.*` skills; planner is read-only by design |
1734:| Plan-cache persistence (Redis / multi-instance) (§22.3) | BUILD-WHEN-SIGNAL | `planner.llm_skipped_rate` drift observed across instances (deployment mode dependent) |
1735:| Schema-change-driven cache invalidation (§9.2) | DEFER-V2 | 60s TTL is sufficient in v1; revisit if TTL-drift causes real staleness incidents |
1736:| Batch-email approval card (one approval, N sends) (§15.4) | DEFER-V2 | Requires a new batch `crm.send_email_batch` action slug in `actionRegistry`; v1 ships single-contact only |
1737:| `topAliasSimilarity` analytics on `planner.stage1_missed` (§17.1) | BUILD-WHEN-SIGNAL | Surfacing near-miss aliases is useful only once Stage 1 coverage plateaus below target |
1738:| Dependency-cruiser rule for read-only enforcement (§22.2) | WON'T-DO (closed) | Replaced by `scripts/verify-crm-query-planner-read-only.sh` static gate; no dependency-cruiser adoption required |
1739:| Stopgap "Ask CRM" standalone panel (§3 context) | WON'T-DO | Brief surface is the CRM question UX; no second entry point |
1740:| Per-subaccount rate-limit budget (§2.1 brief §11.5) | BUILD-WHEN-SIGNAL | Real traffic surfaces cross-subaccount starvation on `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')` |
1741:
1742:Each entry has a verdict and a trigger or reason. Items with `BUILD-WHEN-SIGNAL` include the specific metric or condition that would unblock them — absence of that signal is the reason not to ship now.
1743:
1744:---
1745:
1746:## 23. Risks
1747:
1748:| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
1749:|---|---|---|---|
1750:| Stage 3 LLM produces plans the validator keeps rejecting, yielding high `ambiguous_intent` rates | M | M | Pressure test (§20.3) exposes this before P2 ships; add few-shot examples to prompt if needed |
1751:| Canonical handler extensions don't match existing drizzle schema shape | M | L | P1 catches this at implementation; blocker is isolated to that one handler |
1752:| Hybrid executor's 10-call cap fires on legitimate queries, frustrating users | L | M | `hybrid_unsupported_rate` metric catches this; raise cap if real queries are consistently below cap but still failing |
1753:| LLM cost exceeds budget before `planner.llm_skipped_rate` improves | M | M | Per-query cent ceiling (§16.2) is the circuit breaker; `runCostBreaker` is the backstop |
1754:| Real operator phrasing doesn't match conservative v1 alias list, Stage 1 hit rate is low | H | M | Pressure test + alias-list tuning; synonym map is the main lever. First 2 weeks of real traffic will reveal the biggest misses |
1755:| Planner-vs-Orchestrator boundary drift (implementer teaches Orchestrator to pre-classify) | L | H | Brief §1 + this spec §3 explicitly state the boundary. `pr-reviewer` catches violations. KNOWLEDGE.md entry pins the rule |
1756:| Schema-context injection bloats context window, Stage 3 latency climbs | M | L | Token budget (§16.1) caps injection; pressure test measures actual sizes |
1757:| Query Memory Layer assumptions in event schema turn out wrong when we actually build it | L | L | v2's problem; v1 captures all the obvious fields, v2 can extend |
1758:
1759:---
1760:
1761:## 24. Success criteria
1762:
1763:A senior reviewer can answer yes to all of:
1764:
1765:1. **The planner is the single entry point for CRM reads.** No surface — chat, agent tool call, direct API — reads CRM data without going through the planner.
1766:2. **Deterministic-first is measurable.** `planner.llm_skipped_rate` reports above 0 on day one of P3, and rises over time as aliases and cache accumulate.
1767:3. **All 8 registry entries resolve via Stage 1 for their primary phrasings.** Every alias in the registry hits the registry-backed matcher.
1768:4. **Stage 3 LLM output always passes or cleanly fails.** No in-between states reach the executor. Validator authority is observable via `planner.validation_failed` vs `planner.classified` event counts.
1769:5. **Read-only is statically enforced.** No write helper is in scope for the live executor. Attempted write-helper imports fail `scripts/verify-crm-query-planner-read-only.sh` at CI, before any runtime code executes.
1770:6. **Cost is bounded per query and per run.** Per-query cent ceiling (§16.2) is checked post-Stage-3 — after parse + any escalation — and fires before executor dispatch; per-run ceiling is the backstop via `runCostBreaker` and is checked both before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch.
1771:7. **Failure is a first-class state.** Every error result carries `suggestions[]` that are valid follow-up Briefs. No dead ends.
1772:8. **Rate-limit fair-queueing holds.** ClientPulse polling and planner live queries both use `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')`; neither starves the other.
1773:9. **Observability feeds the future learning loop.** Event payloads include `stageResolved`, `canonicalCandidateKey`, `intentHash`, `hitCount` — the data a Query Memory Layer needs to identify promotion candidates.
1774:10. **Pressure test run and documented.** Results written to `tasks/builds/crm-query-planner/pressure-test-results.md` before P2 ships; any alias / synonym / prompt tunings committed alongside the code.
1775:11. **No schema changes.** `db/schema/` unchanged; migration count unchanged.
1776:12. **No new governance primitives.** `llmRouter`, `runCostBreaker`, `getProviderRateLimiter`, `withPrincipalContext` all reused as-is.
1777:
1778:If yes to all twelve, P3 is ready for `pr-reviewer` and PR to main.
1779:

tasks\builds\crm-query-planner\spec.md:1135:The `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl').acquire()` call queues if the per-location bucket is full. ClientPulse polling and planner live queries share the same bucket — the rate limiter's scheduler (already shipped) ensures fair-queueing so neither starves the other. The planner does not set a timeout on the acquire itself; if the queue depth is pathological, the eventual response will be cost-exceeded or rate-limited depending on which limit fires first.

tasks\builds\crm-query-planner\spec.md:1351:### 16.2 `runCostBreaker`
tasks\builds\crm-query-planner\spec.md:1353:Every planner invocation runs inside a `runId` context (provided by the caller). `runCostBreaker`'s existing `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` is called before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch — the planner does not need new budget logic. If the run's ledger sum exceeds the ceiling, the call throws and the planner surfaces `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }`.
tasks\builds\crm-query-planner\spec.md:1355:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.
tasks\builds\crm-query-planner\spec.md:1359:`getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')` acquired in the live executor (§13.5). Per-location keying matches ClientPulse polling's keying, so the two share the same bucket and fair-queue together.
tasks\builds\crm-query-planner\spec.md:1365:Every canonical read happens inside `withPrincipalContext(context.orgId, context.subaccountId, context.userId, async () => { ... })`. This is existing repo convention — any query outside principal context leaks cross-subaccount data. The planner's caller (`crmQueryPlannerService`) wraps the entire pipeline in this context before dispatching to any executor.

No architectural changes. **Pass 3 summary:** external-review pass 3 applied (merge-ready cleanup). Changes: `agent_run_prompts` gains surrogate `id uuid PK` so `linked_entity_id uuid` on events can reference prompts like every other entity (composite `(run_id, assembly_number)` stays as UNIQUE for the drilldown endpoint); `run.event_limit_reached` gets an exactly-once atomic-claim mechanism via new `agent_runs.event_limit_reached_emitted` column (closes the race where multiple non-critical events arrive at the cap boundary concurrently); `resolveAgentRunVisibility` returns `canViewPayload` (singular) to align with `PermissionMask.canViewPayload`; P3.1 restore contract split explicitly into P3-ships (schema support: `archive_restored_at` column + grace-window logic) vs P3.1-ships (trigger endpoint + worker, deferred until real request); duplicated test block in §10.1 removed. Changes: sequence allocation moved to `agent_runs.next_event_seq` (kills the `MAX + 1` scan + lock); graded failure handling (critical events retry once inline + emit drop metric; non-critical stay fire-and-forget); hard per-run event cap + `run.event_limit_reached` signal event; per-row payload size cap + truncation metadata; `permissionMask` moved to read-time computation (closes a privilege-drift security bug — stored events no longer carry stale authorisation state); payload-table gains `modifications` column separating truncation from redaction; tool-level `payloadPersistencePolicy` for secret-handling skills; batched linked-entity label resolution on snapshot read; minimum restore-contract sketch for cold archive; emission-ordering semantics clarified; every event now carries `sourceService` + `durationSinceRunStartMs`. tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:31:- **Phase C — LLM router cost-breaker wire-up.** Call `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger()` (new in Phase C — see §4.3 and §8.3) from `llmRouter.routeCall()` immediately after each LLM cost is recorded. The breaker primitive already exists in `server/lib/runCostBreaker.ts` (T23) and is called by Slack and Whisper services via the existing `assertWithinRunBudget()` (which reads `cost_aggregates`); the new `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger()` sibling reads `llm_requests` directly to avoid rollup lag (see §7.4.1). `assertWithinRunBudget()` remains scoped to Slack/Whisper; the LLM path uses the ledger variant. Adding the LLM caller closes the dominant cost surface for runaway agent loops.
tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:41:3. **Cost caps that actually cap.** Hermes exposes token/cost ceilings as first-class configuration. The audit confirmed we store `subaccountAgents.maxCostPerRunCents` and built the `runCostBreaker` primitive; but the primary cost-incurring surface (LLM calls) does not call the breaker — only Slack and Whisper services do. A runaway agentic loop today can blow past the configured cap.

tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:127:| Modify | `server/services/llmRouter.ts` | After the cost row has been written to `llm_requests` inside `routeCall`, call `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger({ runId, insertedLedgerRowId, subaccountAgentId, organisationId, correlationId })` when `runId` is present (see §7.4.1 — direct-ledger read path). `insertedLedgerRowId` is captured from the ledger insert's `.returning({ id })` result; the helper fails closed if it's missing or not visible (§7.3.1 invariant). Skip the call when `runId` is null (system / analyzer callers have no run context). The call is dynamic-imported to match the pattern in `sendToSlackService.ts:94`. |
tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:128:| Modify | `server/lib/runCostBreaker.ts` | (a) Existing exported signatures unchanged — `resolveRunCostCeiling`, `getRunCostCents`, `assertWithinRunBudget` keep their current shapes. Slack (`sendToSlackService.ts:94`) and Whisper (`transcribeAudioService.ts:72`) continue to call `assertWithinRunBudget`, which reads from `cost_aggregates`. (b) **Add a new pair of sibling exports** — `getRunCostCentsFromLedger(runId): Promise<number>` (reads `SUM(cost_with_margin_cents) FROM llm_requests WHERE run_id = $1 AND status IN ('success', 'partial')` directly) and `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger(ctx)` (identical to `assertWithinRunBudget` in shape and throw behaviour but internally calls `getRunCostCentsFromLedger` instead of `getRunCostCents`). Only the LLM caller uses the new helpers. Rationale pinned in §7.4.1 — `cost_aggregates` is updated asynchronously by `routerJobService.enqueueAggregateUpdate` (see `llmRouter.ts:897`), so a rollup read would give a stale snapshot and inflate the worst-case overshoot by up to the aggregation lag. The direct-ledger read eliminates that rollup-lag window; the residual concurrency overshoot (bounded by the inflight batch × per-call cost, not by one call's cost — see §7.4 and §11.4 #9) remains because there is no per-run serialization around the insert-and-check sequence. (c) Add a JSDoc line on both `assertWithinRunBudget` and `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` naming their canonical callers — the existing helper lists Slack + Whisper; the new helper lists `llmRouter.routeCall`. |

(b) §552 already lists `runCostBreaker` callers for Slack/Whisper; add LLM router. (c) §1407 run cost circuit breaker section — add LLM router to the caller table. |

tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:626:The pseudocode above makes one thing a type-level contract rather than a comment: `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` takes `insertedLedgerRowId: string | null` as a **required** parameter. The helper implements the following invariant:
tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:629:export async function assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger(
tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:687:**Implementation note — atomic aggregate.** The pseudocode above shows the visibility check and the SUM as two separate statements for readability. The shipped implementation in `server/lib/runCostBreaker.ts::assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` merges them into a single aggregate query: one scan returns both `SUM(cost_with_margin_cents)` and `COALESCE(MAX(CASE WHEN id = $insertedId THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), 0)` under the same `WHERE run_id = $runId AND status IN ('success','partial')` predicate. The breaker decision is therefore based on a single atomic aggregate that simultaneously verifies ledger visibility and computes spend; failure of either condition results in fail-closed behaviour. Future maintainers should not split this back into two queries — the merged shape (a) removes the race window between the visibility SELECT and the SUM SELECT, (b) enforces that the inserted row matches the same `(run_id, counted_status)` predicate as the SUM (catching cross-run contamination and caller misuse), and (c) halves the per-call DB round-trips.
tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:717:The concurrency overshoot claim in §7.4 point 2 holds only if the breaker reads from a data source that has synchronously observed the row we just wrote. The existing `getRunCostCents` implementation (`runCostBreaker.ts:83`) reads from `cost_aggregates` — a rollup table. Two possibilities:
tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:726:Implementation surface (pinned): `runCostBreaker.ts` gains **two new sibling exports** — `getRunCostCentsFromLedger(runId)` and `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger(ctx)` — with signatures identical to the existing `getRunCostCents` / `assertWithinRunBudget` pair but backed by a direct `llm_requests` sum. The LLM caller (`llmRouter.routeCall`) calls `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger`; Slack and Whisper continue to call the unchanged `assertWithinRunBudget`. No `source` parameter on the existing helpers — adding two named siblings is clearer than branching on an enum argument and makes the two data-source contracts independently documentable. See §4.3 row on `runCostBreaker.ts` and §8.3 `runCostBreaker` exports list.
tasks\hermes-audit-tier-1-spec.md:752:**Where the lookup lives.** The IEE → agent-run resolution is a local helper `resolveRunIdFromIee(ctx): Promise<string | null>` defined inline in `server/services/llmRouter.ts` alongside the Phase C breaker call (§7.3 pseudocode already references it). The helper runs a single `SELECT agent_run_id FROM iee_runs WHERE id = $1 LIMIT 1` against the existing index on `iee_runs.id` (primary key). This does not require modifying `iee_runs` schema, adding a new shared utility, or touching `runCostBreaker.ts` — it is a router-local read. The helper is kept inside `llmRouter.ts` rather than promoted to `runCostBreaker.ts` because (a) it is specific to the router's source-type routing, (b) the breaker stays agnostic about how its `runId` was derived, and (c) the router already owns `iee_runs` reads for other routing metadata.

tasks\review-logs\dual-review-log-llm-observability-ledger-2026-04-20T115800Z.md:15:  Reason: REAL logic bug introduced in this branch. When assertCalledFromRouter() is called from an adapter's call() method, the adapter's own file is always in the call stack and matches PROVIDER_FRAME_PATTERN. So hasRouterOrProviderFrame is always true — the guard never throws. There is no intra-provider fallback in this codebase (providerAdapter.call() has only one caller: llmRouter.ts). Fix: removed PROVIDER_FRAME_PATTERN entirely; guard checks only ROUTER_FRAME_PATTERN.
tasks\review-logs\dual-review-log-llm-observability-ledger-2026-04-20T115800Z.md:37:3. Direct-HTTP regex (B6): Pattern catches string-literal URLs to known provider hostnames. Gaps: template literals, env-variable URLs, dynamic construction — acceptable for a belt-and-suspenders gate. Primary enforcement is the adapter import check + runtime assertCalledFromRouter(). New providers must be added to both PROVIDER_NAMES and the hostname list.
tasks\review-logs\dual-review-log-live-agent-execution-log-2026-04-21T212500Z.md:12:  Reason: `tryEmitAgentEvent` is non-blocking. tasks\review-logs\dual-review-log-live-agent-execution-log-2026-04-21T212500Z.md:25:  Reason: The existing `GET /api/agent-runs/:id` route in `agentRuns.ts` has the identical access model (org-scoped, no subaccount membership check). tasks\review-logs\dual-review-log-hermes-tier-1-20260421T073600Z.md:26:  Reason: When `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` throws `cost_limit_exceeded`, execution jumps past both `commitReservation` (line 1152) and `enqueueAggregateUpdate` (line 1155). The `budgetReservations` row stays `active` indefinitely (the reconciler eventually commits it, but the reconciler does NOT call `enqueueAggregateUpdate`). As a result, `cost_aggregates` never receives the over-budget call's row, so `totalCostCents` in `/api/runs/:runId/cost` and `RunCostPanel` permanently undercounts the triggering call's spend for any breaker-tripped run.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter1-codex-output.txt:1088:The `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl').acquire()` call queues if the per-location bucket is full. The planner does not set a timeout on the acquire itself; if the queue depth is pathological, the eventual response will be cost-exceeded or rate-limited depending on which limit fires first.

The planner has no bypass — and must not attempt one.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter1-codex-output.txt:1293:### 16.2 `runCostBreaker`
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter1-codex-output.txt:1295:Every planner invocation runs inside a `runId` context (provided by the caller). `runCostBreaker`'s existing `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` is called before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch — the planner does not need new budget logic. If the run's ledger sum exceeds the ceiling, the call throws and the planner surfaces `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }`.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter1-codex-output.txt:1297:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). Before Stage 3 dispatch, check accumulated-cost-so-far for *this* query invocation; if exceeded, return `cost_exceeded` error. This prevents one exploratory query inside a larger run from burning its entire share of the ceiling.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter1-codex-output.txt:1301:`getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')` acquired in the live executor (§13.5). Per-location keying matches ClientPulse polling's keying, so the two share the same bucket and fair-queue together.

This is existing repo convention — any query outside principal context leaks cross-subaccount data. The planner's caller (`crmQueryPlannerService`) wraps the entire pipeline in this context before dispatching to any executor.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:1386:### 16.2 `runCostBreaker`
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:1388:Every planner invocation runs inside a `runId` context (provided by the caller). `runCostBreaker`'s existing `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` is called before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch — the planner does not need new budget logic. If the run's ledger sum exceeds the ceiling, the call throws and the planner surfaces `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }`.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:1390:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:1394:`getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')` acquired in the live executor (§13.5). Per-location keying matches ClientPulse polling's keying, so the two share the same bucket and fair-queue together.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:1400:Every canonical read happens inside `withPrincipalContext(context.orgId, context.subaccountId, context.userId, async () => { ... })`. This is existing repo convention — any query outside principal context leaks cross-subaccount data. tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:5840:903:      throw new MissingPermissionError(cap);
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:5855:918:`MissingPermissionError` is a small service-layer error thrown by the executor and caught by `crmQueryPlannerService.ts` → emitted as `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'missing_permission', message }`.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6040:1103:  const rateLimiter = getProviderRateLimiter('ghl');
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6046:1109:    const response = await ghlReadHelpers.query(translated, context);
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6056:1119:`translateToProviderQuery(plan)` is a pure function in `liveExecutor.ts`. It maps the plan's `filters`, `sort`, `limit`, `projection`, `dateContext` to the GHL endpoint's query parameters. Output is a typed `GhlReadQuery` matching the `ghlReadHelpers` input contract.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6072:1135:The `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl').acquire()` call queues if the per-location bucket is full. ClientPulse polling and planner live queries share the same bucket — the rate limiter's scheduler (already shipped) ensures fair-queueing so neither starves the other. The planner does not set a timeout on the acquire itself; if the queue depth is pathological, the eventual response will be cost-exceeded or rate-limited depending on which limit fires first.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6267:1330:All Stage 3 LLM calls route through `llmRouter.routeCall`. This is enforced by the repo-wide static gate `scripts/verify-no-direct-adapter-calls.sh` (exit 1 on direct provider imports) plus the runtime `assertCalledFromRouter()` check inside adapters. The planner has no bypass — and must not attempt one.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6288:1351:### 16.2 `runCostBreaker`
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6290:1353:Every planner invocation runs inside a `runId` context (provided by the caller). `runCostBreaker`'s existing `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` is called before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch — the planner does not need new budget logic. If the run's ledger sum exceeds the ceiling, the call throws and the planner surfaces `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }`.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6292:1355:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6296:1359:`getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')` acquired in the live executor (§13.5). Per-location keying matches ClientPulse polling's keying, so the two share the same bucket and fair-queue together.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6302:1365:Every canonical read happens inside `withPrincipalContext(context.orgId, context.subaccountId, context.userId, async () => { ... })`. This is existing repo convention — any query outside principal context leaks cross-subaccount data. The planner's caller (`crmQueryPlannerService`) wraps the entire pipeline in this context before dispatching to any executor.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6324:1387:2. Calls `tryEmitAgentEvent` (from `server/services/agentExecutionEventEmitter.ts`) so the event surfaces in Agent Live Execution Logs (Phase 1 just shipped).
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6411:1474:The edge check is scoped to the target subaccount via `resolveSubaccount(subaccountId, orgId)` (existing convention).
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6417:1480:  slug: 'crm.query',
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6440:1503:Agent-facing tool. Exposed to any agent whose `capabilityMap` grants `crm.query`. The MCP server's tool catalogue auto-picks this up (existing pattern from `server/mcp/mcpServer.ts`).

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6569:1632:- Rate limiter acquire/release lifecycle → covered by `liveExecutor.test.ts` with a mocked `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl')`; asserts acquire on entry and release on both success and error paths.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6606:1669:Access is gated by the existing skill-permission system: agents need `crm.query` in their `capabilityMap` to invoke. Orgs not yet onboarded to the planner simply don't grant the capability.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6610:1673:Added to `systemSettings` table (existing — no schema change, just new rows):

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6625:1688:This is **capability-grant rollout**, not infrastructure-level staged rollout. Code ships to all instances simultaneously on P3 merge — there is no traffic-shifting, no feature flag, no per-org code path, and no canary deploy. Rollout here means onboarding orgs to the planner one at a time via the existing skill-permission system (granting `crm.query` in an org's `capabilityMap`), which is standard operational practice and not in tension with the codebase's "no staged rollout" posture.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6707:1770:6. **Cost is bounded per query and per run.** Per-query cent ceiling (§16.2) is checked post-Stage-3 — after parse + any escalation — and fires before executor dispatch; per-run ceiling is the backstop via `runCostBreaker` and is checked both before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-hermes-iter4-full-input.txt:161:| Modify | `server/lib/runCostBreaker.ts` | (a) Exported signatures unchanged. (b) Add a direct-`llm_requests`-sum read path used by the LLM caller — either as a new optional `{ source: 'llm_requests' \| 'cost_aggregates' }` parameter on `getRunCostCents` (defaulting to `'cost_aggregates'` for back-compat with Slack/Whisper) or as a new sibling helper `getRunCostCentsFromLedger(runId)` called directly by the router. Rationale pinned in §7.4.1 — `cost_aggregates` is updated asynchronously by `routerJobService.enqueueAggregateUpdate` (see `llmRouter.ts:897`), so a rollup read would give a stale snapshot and inflate the worst-case overshoot by up to the aggregation lag. The direct-ledger read eliminates that rollup-lag window; the residual concurrency overshoot (bounded by the inflight batch × per-call cost, not by one call's cost — see §7.4 and §11.4 #9) remains because there is no per-run serialization around the insert-and-check sequence. (c) Add a JSDoc line on `assertWithinRunBudget` noting `llmRouter.routeCall` as a canonical caller. |

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-hermes-iter4-full-input.txt:654:**Where the lookup lives.** The IEE → agent-run resolution is a local helper `resolveRunIdFromIee(ctx): Promise<string | null>` defined inline in `server/services/llmRouter.ts` alongside the Phase C breaker call (§7.3 pseudocode already references it). The helper runs a single `SELECT agent_run_id FROM iee_runs WHERE id = $1 LIMIT 1` against the existing index on `iee_runs.id` (primary key). This does not require modifying `iee_runs` schema, adding a new shared utility, or touching `runCostBreaker.ts` — it is a router-local read. The helper is kept inside `llmRouter.ts` rather than promoted to `runCostBreaker.ts` because (a) it is specific to the router's source-type routing, (b) the breaker stays agnostic about how its `runId` was derived, and (c) the router already owns `iee_runs` reads for other routing metadata.

The three exports (`resolveRunCostCeiling`, `getRunCostCents`, `assertWithinRunBudget`) keep their public shapes; the LLM caller consumes the new read path. JSDoc on `assertWithinRunBudget` gains a new caller entry.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-llmobs-iter1-codex-output.txt:2189:docs\canonical-data-platform-roadmap.md:288:**Decision.** Every canonical table and every principal/connection table ships with Postgres Row-Level Security policies. **Org-level RLS already exists on this codebase** — Sprint 2 P1.1 (migrations 0079–0082, plus every tenant-table migration since) shipped Layer 1 RLS keyed on `current_setting('app.organisation_id', true)`, with the manifest in `server/config/rlsProtectedTables.ts` and the gates `scripts/gates/verify-rls-coverage.sh` + `verify-rls-contract-compliance.sh`. P3 **extends** that infrastructure with principal-scoped predicates (owner / visibility / principal_type) and adds the `withPrincipalContext` wrapper on top of the existing `withOrgTx` / `getOrgScopedDb` / `withAdminConnection` primitives. Service-layer scope checks (the existing pattern — services require `organisationId` arguments, filter in the query) are retained as defence-in-depth and as the primary error-surface for developers.

1:import { sql } from 'drizzle-orm';
2:import type { PrincipalContext } from '../services/principal/types.js';
3:import { getOrgTxContext } from '../instrumentation.js';
4:import type { OrgScopedTx } from './index.js';
5:
6:/**
7: * Layer principal session variables on top of the existing org-scoped
8: * transaction opened by `withOrgTx`. The org variable
9: * (`app.organisation_id`) is already set by the `withOrgTx` caller;
10: * this function adds the four principal variables that RLS policies
11: * introduced in P3B consume:
12: *
13: *   - `app.current_subaccount_id`
14: *   - `app.current_principal_type`
15: *   - `app.current_principal_id`
16: *   - `app.current_team_ids`
17: *
18: * Must be called inside an active `withOrgTx(...)` block. Throws if
19: * no org-scoped transaction is active.
20: */
21:export async function withPrincipalContext<T>(
22:  principal: PrincipalContext,
23:  work: (tx: OrgScopedTx) => Promise<T>,
24:): Promise<T> {
25:  if (!principal.organisationId) {
26:    throw new Error('withPrincipalContext: principal.organisationId is required');
27:  }
28:
29:  const orgCtx = getOrgTxContext();
30:  if (!orgCtx) {
31:    throw new Error(
32:      'withPrincipalContext: must be called inside an active withOrgTx() block',
33:    );
34:  }
35:
36:  const tx = orgCtx.tx as OrgScopedTx;
37:
38:  // Set all four principal session variables in a single round-trip.
39:  // `true` (is_local) scopes these to the current transaction, matching
40:  // the pattern used by auth middleware for app.organisation_id.
41:  await tx.execute(sql`
42:    SELECT
43:      set_config('app.current_subaccount_id',
44:        ${principal.subaccountId ?? ''}, true),
45:      set_config('app.current_principal_type',
46:        ${principal.type}, true),
47:      set_config('app.current_principal_id',
48:        ${principal.id}, true),
49:      set_config('app.current_team_ids',
50:        ${(principal.teamIds ?? []).join(',')}, true)
51:  `);
52:
53:  return work(tx);
54:}

tasks\builds\crm-query-planner\spec.md:1355:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.

tasks\builds\clientpulse\session-1-foundation-spec.md:753:`config_history` rows written by Phase-4.5 use `entity_type='clientpulse_operational_config'` and `entity_id=hierarchy_template.id`. Post-§2, the target is the organisation, so:
tasks\builds\clientpulse\session-1-foundation-spec.md:755:- **Entity type change:** `clientpulse_operational_config` → `organisation_operational_config`. New rows use the new type. Creation-event rows written by §7.2 step 6 carry `snapshot_after=NULL` — history readers must treat `snapshot_after=NULL` as a creation marker meaning "no explicit overrides yet"; they must not reconstruct a point-in-time JSON snapshot unless a historical defaults snapshot is explicitly available. (The current adopted-template defaults are mutable over time, so the NULL row is a semantic marker, not a reconstruction anchor.)
tasks\builds\clientpulse\session-1-foundation-spec.md:756:- **Existing rows:** leave as-is. The entity_id still points at the (renamed) hierarchy_template row, which now stores the one-time seed. Readers that grep `clientpulse_operational_config` in history continue to find historical entries.
tasks\builds\clientpulse\session-1-foundation-spec.md:761:**Implementation owner for the union + entity-type whitelist:** `server/services/configHistoryService.ts` exports the `CONFIG_HISTORY_ENTITY_TYPES` set used by `server/routes/configHistory.ts` to validate inbound `entityType` query params. Today the set does not include either the legacy `clientpulse_operational_config` or the new `organisation_operational_config` (Phase-4.5 writes go to the table directly without a gate-set check). §4.8's work list therefore adds `clientpulse_operational_config` AND `organisation_operational_config` to the `CONFIG_HISTORY_ENTITY_TYPES` set, and extends the history-read route in `server/routes/configHistory.ts` to accept a special `organisation_config_all` query value that internally `OR`s both entity types so operators see a single contiguous timeline. Both files are listed in the §9.3 modify table.

This is an audit-trail marker for the creation event, not a config-change record; the `snapshot_after=NULL` value is the signal that no override write has happened.
