tasks\builds\clientpulse\session-1-foundation-spec.md:1239:**Data-loss caveat (pre-production framing).** Any writes that land in `organisations.operational_config_override` **after** the forward migration completes are not mirrored back into `hierarchy_templates.operational_config_seed`; a straight `_down` that drops the override column would drop those writes. This is acceptable under the current framing (`live_users: no`, `rollout_model: commit_and_revert` per `docs/spec-context.md`) — dev-environment data is disposable during the rollback window. If Session 1 ships past the first live-user milestone, add a `_down` step that copies each org's `operational_config_override` back into the resolved `hierarchy_templates` row before dropping the column. Until then: **no data-loss risk within the pre-production envelope. Downgrade window: 1 release cycle.**

tasks\review-logs\pr-review-log-clientpulse-session-1-20260420T013000Z.md:86:The Drizzle schema (`server/db/schema/hierarchyTemplates.ts`) renamed the field from `operationalConfig` to `operationalConfigSeed` (which maps to SQL column `operational_config_seed`, renamed by migration 0180). The `.set({ operationalConfig: ... } as Record<string, unknown>)` cast at line 779 and `.values({ ... operationalConfig: ... } as typeof hierarchyTemplates.$inferInsert)` at line 792 bypass TypeScript's ORM type checking via the `as` cast, but the generated SQL will reference the old column name `operational_config` which no longer exists post-migration. This will throw a Postgres column-not-found error any time `applySystemTemplate` is called on an org.

Today the set does not include either the legacy `clientpulse_operational_config` or the new `organisation_operational_config` (Phase-4.5 writes go to the table directly without a gate-set check). §4.8's work list therefore adds `clientpulse_operational_config` AND `organisation_operational_config` to the `CONFIG_HISTORY_ENTITY_TYPES` set, and extends the history-read route in `server/routes/configHistory.ts` to accept a special `organisation_config_all` query value that internally `OR`s both entity types so operators see a single contiguous timeline. tasks\review-logs\_session-1-foundation-iter5-codex-output.txt:1039:6. This is an audit-trail marker for the creation event, not a config-change record; the `snapshot_after=NULL` value is the signal that no override write has happened.

tasks\review-logs\_session-1-foundation-iter5-codex-output.txt:1212:**Data-loss caveat (pre-production framing).** Any writes that land in `organisations.operational_config_override` **after** the forward migration completes are not mirrored back into `hierarchy_templates.operational_config_seed`; a straight `_down` that drops the override column would drop those writes. This is acceptable under the current framing (`live_users: no`, `rollout_model: commit_and_revert` per `docs/spec-context.md`) — dev-environment data is disposable during the rollback window. If Session 1 ships past the first live-user milestone, add a `_down` step that copies each org's `operational_config_override` back into the resolved `hierarchy_templates` row before dropping the column. Until then: **no data-loss risk within the pre-production envelope. Downgrade window: 1 release cycle.**

Per §6.5 the Subaccount Blueprint editor renders the seed block read-only as a reference display, not as an input to any live code path.

Until then: **no data-loss risk within the pre-production envelope. Downgrade window: 1 release cycle.**

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-full-input.txt:1377:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-crm-query-planner-iter2-codex-output.txt:6292:1355:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-iter2-codex-output.txt:9376:3. **§4.8 / §9.3 — config-history continuity has no implementation owner**. The spec says history readers must union `clientpulse_operational_config` and `organisation_operational_config`, but the inventory omits `server/services/configHistoryService.ts` and `server/routes/configHistory.ts`; in the current repo, `CONFIG_HISTORY_ENTITY_TYPES` rejects both. Suggested fix: add those files to §9.3 and state exactly where the union lives; at minimum update `configHistoryService.ts` and `configHistory.ts` so both entity types are accepted/readable.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-iter2-codex-output.txt:9386:8. **§6.5 / S1-5.2 — blueprint visibility contract contradicts itself**. S1-5.2 says no operational-config fields are visible, but §6.5 also requires a read-only preview of the `operational_config_seed` block. Suggested fix: pick one contract and make both sections match; the minimal edit is to change S1-5.2/manual verification to “no editable operational-config fields are visible” if the preview stays.

tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-iter3-codex-output.txt:1361:1. **§7.2 — `createFromTemplate` seeds the override row with full defaults**. Step 2 says `systemHierarchyTemplates.operational_defaults` is copied wholesale into `organisations.operational_config_override`, but §2/§4/§6 treat that column as sparse org overrides whose path presence drives override badges and reset affordances. A full copy would mark every field as overridden on day one and shadow future system-template default changes. Suggested fix: edit §7.2 step 2 so create-org sets `applied_system_template_id` and leaves `operational_config_override` `NULL` (or writes only true org-specific deltas), then update §7.1/§8.4 verification text to match.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-iter3-codex-output.txt:1365:3. **§2.4 / §8.8 — rollback does not preserve post-migration config writes**. The rollback text says the backfilled data is recoverable from `hierarchy_templates.operational_config_seed`, but that only covers the pre-migration snapshot. Any post-deploy writes land only in `organisations.operational_config_override` and are lost when that column is dropped. Suggested fix: make the `_down` for `NNNN_org_operational_config_override.sql` copy each org’s current `operational_config_override` back into the resolved `hierarchy_templates` row before renaming/dropping columns, and update §8.8’s “No data loss risk” wording accordingly.
tasks\review-logs\_spec-review-iter3-codex-output.txt:1367:4. **§2.2 / §6.5 / S1-5.2 / §7.2 — subaccount-seeding source-of-truth is inconsistent**. §2.2 says blueprints keep `operational_config_seed` so they can still carry seed defaults; S1-5.2 says new subaccounts should seed from the org’s current override; §7.2 step 3 says `operational_config_seed` is populated “for reference only.” Those are different contracts. Suggested fix: pick one source explicitly and align all sections. If the intended source is the live org config, update §2.2, §6.5, §7.2 step 3, and S1-5.2 to say subaccount creation reads `organisations.operational_config_override` plus system defaults, and `operational_config_seed` is informational only.

664:- **Only validated plans** (`stageResolved === 3`) are written to cache. Stage 1 hits are already free and don't need caching. Stage 2 hits don't re-cache (the entry is already present).

1519:**What ships:** the deterministic part of the planner. Stages 1, 2, 4; canonical executor; canonical registry with all 8 entries; result normaliser; API route; skill registration. **Stage 3 is stubbed** — on any intent that misses Stage 1 and Stage 2, the stub short-circuits to a `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'unsupported_query', suggestions: [...] }` **before** Stage 4 runs. The cache module (§9) is shipped but its write path is never exercised in P1 because only Stage 3 successes are cached and Stage 3 produces none. Stage 2 reads are wired so P2's first successful Stage 3 can populate the cache without further plumbing.

1552:**Hybrid handling in P2:** the hybrid executor (§14) does NOT ship until P3. Until P3, Stage 4's hybrid-pattern rule (§11.2 rule 7) still runs to detect the shape, but any plan with `source: 'hybrid'` is rewritten by `crmQueryPlannerService` into a `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'unsupported_query', suggestions: [...'ships in P3...'] }` before executor dispatch. This keeps Stage 4's shape-check logic exercised during P2 without requiring the executor half.

codex
I’ve confirmed one major source-of-truth mismatch: the spec’s proposed `crm.query` action entry doesn’t look like the real `ACTION_REGISTRY` schema at all. I’m pulling the router/provider response types too, because the spec also appears to assume `routeCall()` returns parsed objects and ledger entries directly, which may not match the live router contract.

 succeeded in 378ms:
1:/**
2: * Token-bucket rate limiter for external API calls.
3: * In-memory for MVP (not shared across server instances).
4: *
5: * Usage:
6: *   const limiter = new RateLimiter({ maxTokens: 100, refillRate: 10, refillIntervalMs: 1000 });
7: *   await limiter.acquire('account-123'); // waits if bucket is empty
8: */
9:
10:interface BucketState {
11:  tokens: number;
12:  lastRefill: number;
13:}
14:
15:interface RateLimiterConfig {
16:  /** Maximum tokens in the bucket */
17:  maxTokens: number;
18:  /** Tokens added per refill interval */
19:  refillRate: number;
20:  /** Milliseconds between refills */
21:  refillIntervalMs: number;
22:  /** Optional callback when approaching limit (< 20% tokens remaining) */
23:  onThreshold?: (key: string, remaining: number) => void;
24:}
25:
26:export class RateLimiter {
27:  private buckets = new Map<string, BucketState>();
28:  private config: RateLimiterConfig;
29:
30:  constructor(config: RateLimiterConfig) {
31:    this.config = config;
32:  }
33:
34:  /**
35:   * Acquire a token from the bucket for the given key.
36:   * Waits if no tokens are available. Returns when token is consumed.
37:   */
38:  async acquire(key: string): Promise<void> {
39:    const bucket = this.getOrCreateBucket(key);
40:    this.refill(bucket);
41:
42:    if (bucket.tokens >= 1) {
43:      bucket.tokens -= 1;
44:
45:      // Threshold warning
46:      if (this.config.onThreshold && bucket.tokens < this.config.maxTokens * 0.2) {
47:        this.config.onThreshold(key, bucket.tokens);
48:      }
49:      return;
50:    }
51:
52:    // Wait for next refill
53:    const waitMs = this.config.refillIntervalMs - (Date.now() - bucket.lastRefill);
54:    await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, Math.max(waitMs, 100)));
55:    return this.acquire(key); // Retry after wait
56:  }
57:
58:  /**
59:   * Check if a token is available without consuming it.
60:   */
61:  canAcquire(key: string): boolean {
62:    const bucket = this.getOrCreateBucket(key);
63:    this.refill(bucket);
64:    return bucket.tokens >= 1;
65:  }
66:
67:  /**
68:   * Get remaining tokens for a key.
69:   */
70:  remaining(key: string): number {
71:    const bucket = this.getOrCreateBucket(key);
72:    this.refill(bucket);
73:    return Math.floor(bucket.tokens);
74:  }
75:
76:  private getOrCreateBucket(key: string): BucketState {
77:    let bucket = this.buckets.get(key);
78:    if (!bucket) {
79:      bucket = { tokens: this.config.maxTokens, lastRefill: Date.now() };
80:      this.buckets.set(key, bucket);
81:    }
82:    return bucket;
83:  }
84:
85:  private refill(bucket: BucketState): void {
86:    const now = Date.now();
87:    const elapsed = now - bucket.lastRefill;
88:    const intervals = Math.floor(elapsed / this.config.refillIntervalMs);
89:    if (intervals > 0) {
90:      bucket.tokens = Math.min(this.config.maxTokens, bucket.tokens + intervals * this.config.refillRate);
91:      bucket.lastRefill = now;
92:    }
93:  }
94:}
95:

98:// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

101:  ghl: {
102:    maxTokens: 100,
103:    refillRate: 100,
104:    refillIntervalMs: 10_000, // 100 requests per 10 seconds per location
105:    onThreshold: (key, remaining) => {
106:      console.warn(`[GHL RateLimiter] Approaching rate limit for ${key}: ${remaining} tokens remaining`);
107:    },
108:  },
109:  teamwork: {
110:    maxTokens: 150,
111:    refillRate: 150,
112:    refillIntervalMs: 60_000, // Teamwork Desk: 150 requests per minute
113:    onThreshold: (key, remaining) => {
114:      console.warn(`[Teamwork RateLimiter] Approaching rate limit for ${key}: ${remaining} tokens remaining`);
115:    },
116:  },
117:  slack: {
118:    maxTokens: 50,
119:    refillRate: 50,
120:    refillIntervalMs: 60_000, // Slack Web API: ~50 requests per minute (tier 2)
121:    onThreshold: (key, remaining) => {
122:      console.warn(`[Slack RateLimiter] Approaching rate limit for ${key}: ${remaining} tokens remaining`);
123:    },
124:  },
125:};

132: * Falls back to a conservative default for unknown providers.
133: */
134:export function getProviderRateLimiter(provider: string): RateLimiter {
135:  let limiter = providerLimiters.get(provider);

1:// Fire-and-forget wrapper around appendEvent ? intended for use at
2:// emission sites inside agent-side services. Swallows every error and
3:// never throws into the caller. The append service already retries
4:// critical events + records metrics/logs on drop; this helper only
5:// exists to keep emission-site boilerplate to one line.
6://
7:// Usage:
8://
9://   import { tryEmitAgentEvent } from './agentExecutionEventEmitter.js';
10://
11://   tryEmitAgentEvent({
12://     runId,
13://     organisationId,
14://     subaccountId,
15://     sourceService: 'workspaceMemoryService',
16://     payload: { eventType: 'memory.retrieved', critical: false, ... },
17://     linkedEntity: { type: 'memory_entry', id: top.id },
18://   });
19://
20:// The emission is `await`ed internally but the returned promise is
21:// discarded by default ? callers that want to hold the agent loop until
22:// the emit completes can use `await emitAgentEvent(...)` instead.
23:
24:import { appendEvent, type AppendEventInput } from './agentExecutionEventService.js';
25:import { logger } from '../lib/logger.js';
26:
27:/** Fire-and-forget emission. Never throws into the caller. */
28:export function tryEmitAgentEvent(input: AppendEventInput): void {
29:  void emitAgentEvent(input);
30:}
31:
32:/** Awaitable emission ? use from paths that genuinely want to block. */
33:export async function emitAgentEvent(input: AppendEventInput): Promise<void> {
34:  try {
35:    await appendEvent(input);
36:  } catch (err) {
37:    // appendEvent itself should never throw in P1 (it catches its own
38:    // retry failures). If something slips through, log it and keep
39:    // going ? the log-table write must never fail the agent run.
40:    logger.warn('agentExecutionEventEmitter.unexpected_throw', {
41:      runId: input.runId,
42:      eventType: input.payload.eventType,
43:      err: err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err),
44:    });
45:  }
46:}

1353:Every planner invocation runs inside a `runId` context (provided by the caller). `runCostBreaker`'s existing `assertWithinRunBudgetFromLedger` is called before Stage 3 and before live-executor dispatch — the planner does not need new budget logic. If the run's ledger sum exceeds the ceiling, the call throws and the planner surfaces `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }`.
1355:**Per-query cent ceiling (new — local to planner):** independent of per-run. System default `100` cents (configurable via `systemSettings.crm_query_planner_per_query_cents`). The check fires **after Stage 3 completes** (including any escalation retry, per §10.3), before the validator dispatches to the executor: sum the cost of Stage 3's initial parse + escalation retry (if any) via `llmRouter`'s returned ledger entries, and if the sum exceeds the ceiling, short-circuit with `BriefErrorResult { errorCode: 'cost_exceeded' }` without executor dispatch. Pre-Stage-3 there is zero accumulated planner cost — the ceiling is inherently a post-escalation guard. This prevents one exploratory query that escalates to the high tier from burning its entire share of the run's ceiling.

codex
1. `QuerySource` / `DraftQueryPlan` still cannot represent the `unsupported` branch the spec asks Stage 3 to emit. In `spec.md:295-310`, `QuerySource` is only `'canonical' | 'live' | 'hybrid'`, and `DraftQueryPlan` in `spec.md:387` inherits that shape, but the prompt in `spec.md:726` tells the model to set `source='unsupported'`. That is a hard type contradiction, not just underspecification.

2. The spec still names several existing primitives with the wrong contract, so it is not implementation-ready against the current repo surface. `spec.md:691-704` assumes `llmRouter.routeCall` takes `schema:` and returns a parsed `DraftQueryPlan`, but the real router takes `postProcess?: (content: string) => void | Promise<void>` and returns `ProviderResponse` with string `content` (`server/services/llmRouter.ts:97-120`, `server/services/providers/types.ts:41-47`). `spec.md:1098-1114` assumes `getProviderRateLimiter('ghl').acquire(...)` returns a releaser and that `ghlReadHelpers.query(...)` / `GhlReadQuery` exist, but the real limiter is `acquire(key): Promise<void>` with no release (`server/lib/rateLimiter.ts:35-55`) and `ghlReadHelpers` currently exposes specific `listGhl*` helpers, not a generic `query()` (`server/services/adapters/ghlReadHelpers.ts:147-186`).

3. The RLS/principal-context reuse claim is still wrong as written. `spec.md:1365` treats `withPrincipalContext` as a standalone wrapper over the full planner pipeline, but the real primitive is `withPrincipalContext(principal, work)` and it must run inside an active `withOrgTx(...)` block (`server/db/withPrincipalContext.ts:18-24, 29-33`). This is a load-bearing enforcement claim with the wrong existing mechanism.

4. P1 sequencing is still broken around Stage 1 validation. `spec.md:612-622` says Stage 1 hits run a reduced validator subset against the caller’s current `schemaContext`, but `schemaContextService.ts` / `schemaContextPure.ts` do not ship until P2 (`spec.md:1559-1560`), while P1 still claims Stages 1, 2, and 4 are shippable (`spec.md:1519`). Either schema-context construction is a P1 dependency, or Stage 1 cannot enforce the stated invariant in P1.

5. The cache contract still violates the permission invariant the validator claims to enforce. `spec.md:836` says projection overlap is checked against the caller’s principal context, but `validatePlanPure` only takes `DraftQueryPlan + SchemaContext + CanonicalQueryRegistry` (`spec.md:808`), and Stage 2 cache keys only on `subaccountId + hash` (`spec.md:647-650`). That allows a plan validated for one principal to be reused by another principal in the same subaccount with a different field-visibility envelope.

6. The canonical-promotion rule still contradicts the canonical executor contract. `spec.md:831` says a live plan with zero live-only filters is promoted to canonical and drops `canonicalCandidateKey`, but `canonicalExecutor` immediately throws if a canonical plan lacks that key (`spec.md:891-918`). That tie-breaker cannot dispatch successfully as written.

7. The hybrid unsupported metric is still poisoned by the phase plan. `spec.md:1416` defines `planner.hybrid_unsupported_rate` as the signal for adding new hybrid patterns, but P2 intentionally rewrites every hybrid plan to `unsupported_query` until P3 (`spec.md:1552-1554`). During P2, that metric measures “executor not shipped yet,” not “pattern gap,” so the Deferred Items trigger in `spec.md:1725` is not trustworthy.

8. Route gating and rollout gating still describe two different access models. The route allows any caller with any CRM read capability (`spec.md:1472`), while the rollout section says orgs are onboarded by granting `crm.query` in the agent `capabilityMap` (`spec.md:1503`, `spec.md:1669`, `spec.md:1688-1692`). As written, the direct route bypasses the stated rollout mechanism, and the request body in `spec.md:1450-1454` also lets the caller supply `orgId`, `subaccountId`, and `userId` instead of treating them as auth-derived context.

9. The new `crm.query` action entry still does not match the real `ACTION_REGISTRY` schema. The spec’s proposed object in `spec.md:1480-1499` uses `slug`, `namespace`, `category`, and `gateLevel`; the real registry uses `actionType`, `actionCategory`, `defaultGateLevel`, `readPath`, `createsBoardTask`, `parameterSchema`, etc. (`server/config/actionRegistry.ts:54-71`, `server/config/actionRegistry.ts:117`). This is concrete contract drift against an existing source of truth.

10. The cost surface still has no single owner. `QueryPlan` carries `costPreview` (`spec.md:383`), the API response also carries top-level `costPreview` (`spec.md:1464-1465`), observability emits `predictedCostCents` (`spec.md:1403`), and `spec.md:1355` says the per-query ceiling is computed from “`llmRouter`’s returned ledger entries” even though the actual router returns `ProviderResponse`, not ledger rows (`server/services/llmRouter.ts:262`, `server/services/providers/types.ts:41-47`). This needs one named calculator and one source-of-truth field.

11. §5 still has file-inventory drift. The prose requires updating `server/index.ts` to mount the new route (`server/index.ts:234-338` pattern), updating `server/services/systemSettingsService.ts` because system-setting writes are allowlisted through `SETTING_KEYS` (`server/services/systemSettingsService.ts:21`, `server/routes/systemSettings.ts:15-20`), and updating `scripts/run-all-gates.sh` because `spec.md:1123` says the new verify script is added there. None of those files appear in the §5 layout.

12. A few lower-severity iteration-2 drifts remain:
- `spec.md:549` mentions a `normalisedAt` field on `NormalisedIntent`, but the type in `spec.md:281-291` has no such field.
- `spec.md:111` points to alias seed list in §12.3, but the registry entries are in §12.2.
- `spec.md:1659` says “see §21 open questions,” but open questions are §22.
- Approval-card scope still flips between plural result-set wording (`spec.md:60`, `spec.md:101`) and the narrowed single-contact-only rule (`spec.md:1259-1269`, `spec.md:1736`).
- P3 says “any remaining approval card patterns” ship (`spec.md:1576`), which conflicts with the closed decision that v1 ships exactly one illustrative approval-card example.

Main pattern: the second pass fixed a lot of prose, but the spec still has several source-of-truth mismatches against existing primitives plus two real sequencing bugs. I would not call it implementation-ready until those are resolved.
